posts.title: Why Your Marathon Pace Is Wrong on Hilly Courses
posts.excerpt: Running even splits on a hilly course doesn't mean even effort. Here's the math behind grade-adjusted pacing and why it changes how you should race Boston, Big Sur, and NYC.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Why Your Marathon Pace Is Wrong on Hilly Courses

If you run 5:41 per km for a 4:00 marathon, that pace works on a flat course. On Boston, it doesn't. Not because you're slower, but because 5:41 on a hill costs more energy than 5:41 on flat ground.

This is the thing most runners get wrong about hilly courses. They pick a target pace, run it from the gun, and wonder why they blow up at mile 20. The pace wasn't the problem. Running that pace at equal energy output on every terrain was.

## Even Pace vs Even Effort

Flat road running is simple. Distance divided by time gives you pace. Pace times energy cost gives you fuel burned. Everything scales linearly.

Hills break this. Running uphill at your goal pace requires significantly more oxygen than running that same pace on flat ground. The reverse is true on downhills, though with diminishing returns on steep descents because of the braking forces involved.

So if you lock onto 5:41 per km and hold it across every terrain change, you're burning too hot on the uphills and leaving time on the table on the downhills.

Even effort means your perceived exertion, heart rate, and oxygen consumption stay roughly constant. Your pace will vary with the terrain. That's correct. That's the goal.

## The Math Behind It

There's a formula for this. Physiologist Alberto Minetti published research on the energy cost of running at various grades. The key finding: the relationship between grade and energy cost is not linear.

A 5% uphill grade increases energy cost by roughly 35-40% compared to flat. A 5% downhill grade reduces energy cost by about 20%. The asymmetry matters. Hills cost more than they give back.

This is why net-downhill courses like Boston aren't as fast as you'd expect. The 245m of total descent sounds like free time. But the 156m of gain, arriving in the Newton Hills at mile 16, costs more than the downhill earns.

## What This Means in Practice

For a 4:00 goal at Boston, an even-effort strategy looks something like:

- Miles 1-5 (net downhill): run closer to 5:30-5:35 pace. The hill works for you.
- Miles 16-21 (Newton Hills): expect 6:10-6:30 pace. You're not blowing up. The grade is eating your pace.
- Miles 22-26 (downhill to finish): back toward 5:40-5:50 if you've managed effort correctly.

Your GPS will show wildly different paces throughout. Your body will feel roughly the same effort the entire time. That's the point.

## Boston vs Berlin

Berlin is essentially flat. Every kilometer costs roughly the same energy. A 4:00 goal there really does mean 5:41 per km, held consistently, from start to finish.

Boston requires the same fitness to run 4:00 as running about 3:50-3:52 on a flat course. That's the fitness penalty the Newton Hills extract.

Big Sur is more extreme still. The quad-crushing climbs early in the race mean a 4:00 there might require flat-equivalent fitness of 3:40-3:45.

This is what "course difficulty" actually means, quantified.

## Using the Race Simulator

I built a [Race Simulator](/simulate) that runs these calculations for 15 major marathons and half marathons. You enter a goal time, pick a course, and get a split-by-split pacing strategy that maintains even effort across the actual elevation profile.

It also shows the flat-equivalent time: the fitness required to run your goal on that course. If you're targeting 3:30 at Boston and the simulator says the flat-equivalent fitness needed is 3:20, you now know the gap between what you need and what you have.

The feasibility check compares that number to your VDOT if you enter a recent race time. Conservative, realistic, ambitious, or very aggressive — you know before you stand on the start line.

The course difficulty ranking shows all 15 courses ordered by how much harder they are than a flat race. Boston is tough. Big Sur is brutal. Berlin and Valencia are close to flat. Now you can see exactly by how much.

Stop guessing your pace on hills. The math exists. Use it.

posts.title: How to Build a Training Plan for Your Goal Race (Without Guessing)
posts.excerpt: A periodized marathon training plan isn't magic — it's a structured progression through base building, development, sharpening, and taper. Here's exactly how to build one around your life.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Build a Training Plan for Your Goal Race (Without Guessing)

For the first two years I ran, I didn't have a training plan. I just ran however much felt right, which usually meant alternating between "not enough" and "too much and then injured."

When I finally committed to a structured plan for the half marathon, I dropped 20 minutes in a single training block. The improvement wasn't from running more — I'd been running *more* for years. It was from running the right things, in the right order, at the right time.

Here's what I learned about building a plan that actually works.

## The Four-Phase Model

Every serious training plan — regardless of the race distance — follows a variation of the same four-phase structure:

### 1. Base Building (40% of your training)
This is aerobic foundation work. Almost entirely easy running, with maybe one tempo effort per week. The goal isn't to get fast. The goal is to build the physiological infrastructure that lets you get fast later: capillary density, mitochondrial development, connective tissue resilience.

Base miles are boring. Run them anyway.

### 2. Development (30% of your training)
You start adding structure here — moderate tempo runs, steady long runs with some marathon-pace effort, progressive runs. You're teaching your body to clear lactate at higher speeds.

This is where most amateur runners start. Which is why most amateur runners plateau.

### 3. Sharpening (25% of your training)
Now the hard work begins: VO2max intervals, race-pace efforts, tune-up races. Your aerobic base absorbs these high-intensity sessions without breaking down — because you built it first.

Without the base phase, this is where runners get hurt. With it, this is where runners get fast.

### 4. Taper (5-10% of your training)
Volume drops 30-50%. Intensity stays. Your body consolidates the adaptations from the previous 10-16 weeks. You show up to the start line fresh.

The taper is not a rest period. It's a precision phase.

## How Long Should Your Plan Be?

This depends on your goal race and current fitness:

| Race | Beginner | Intermediate | Advanced |
|------|----------|--------------|----------|
| 5K | 8 weeks | 10 weeks | 12 weeks |
| 10K | 10 weeks | 12 weeks | 14 weeks |
| Half Marathon | 12 weeks | 14 weeks | 16 weeks |
| Marathon | 16 weeks | 18 weeks | 20 weeks |

If you're 8 weeks from your goal race and haven't started training, you're not late — you're just training for a shorter block. Adjust your expectations accordingly, and still follow the phase structure within the time you have.

## The Biggest Mistake: Running All Runs at the Same Pace

This is where most self-coached runners fail. They don't have distinct easy days and hard days — they have a continuous medium effort that produces continuous medium results.

The research is clear: for most recreational runners, 80% of weekly mileage should be easy. Truly easy. The kind of pace where you can hold a conversation, feel slightly bored, and question whether you're working hard enough.

You are. The aerobic adaptations from easy running are cumulative. They accumulate while you're sleeping, while you're sitting at your desk, for 48 hours after the run. All that easy running is silently upgrading your engine.

The other 20% is where you earn race fitness: tempo runs, intervals, long runs with race-pace efforts.

## What Paces Should You Train At?

This is where VDOT comes in. Enter a recent race result into the [VDOT Calculator](/vdot) and you'll get exact paces for each training zone:

- **Easy pace**: conversations happen
- **Marathon pace**: controlled hard
- **Threshold pace**: comfortably uncomfortable
- **Interval pace**: hard but not sprint
- **Repetition pace**: fast

These aren't suggestions. They're scientifically derived from your current fitness. Running faster than your easy pace on easy days doesn't make you fitter — it makes you more tired.

## Building Around Your Life

The best training plan is one you'll actually follow. Before you build the weeks, answer these questions:

**Which days can you reliably run?** Most plans assume 4-5 days per week. If you can only do 4, that's fine — cut the recovery run, keep the long run and two quality sessions.

**What time do you have on the weekend?** The long run is the cornerstone of every plan. Protect Saturday or Sunday. Make the other day secondary.

**When is your "A" race?** Count back from race day. Build your phases from there. Race day is week N. Taper starts N-2 (for 5K/10K) or N-3 (for half/full). Sharpening fills the weeks before that.

## The Free Training Plan Generator

I built [TrainPace's Training Plan Generator](/plan) to automate exactly this process. You tell it:
- Your goal race (5K, 10K, Half, Marathon)
- Your race date
- Your available training days
- Your current fitness level
- Your goal time (optional)

It generates a complete periodized plan — week by week, workout by workout — with paces derived from Jack Daniels' VDOT methodology. If you've already calculated your VDOT on the [VDOT Calculator](/vdot), your training paces carry over automatically.

You can also export the full plan to your calendar as an .ics file, which imports directly into Google Calendar or Apple Calendar. Each workout lands on the correct date with the duration, description, and pace zone included.

## The Most Important Principle

Consistency beats everything.

A 14-week plan where you hit 85% of the workouts will outperform a 20-week plan where you hit 60%. The compounding effect of consistent easy mileage is what drives improvement — not the occasional heroic long run.

Don't skip the easy days because they feel too easy. Don't skip the hard days because they sound intimidating. Show up, execute the session in front of you, and trust the process.

The plan works if you do the plan.

[Build your training plan now →](/plan)

posts.title: How to Actually Research Running Shoes
posts.excerpt: Most shoe advice online is useless. Here's how to figure out what actually works for you.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Actually Research Running Shoes

Most shoe advice online is useless. Review sites get affiliate commissions. YouTube reviewers get free product. Even well-meaning runners repeat things they heard without the mileage to back it up.

Here's what actually works.

## Start with Your Own History

Before looking at anything new, look at what you've already run in.

What worked? What caused problems? If a shoe gave you knee pain after a few months, check if the midsole was too soft for your weight and mileage. If you wore through the heel fast, that's information about your footstrike. If blisters showed up, look at the toe box.

Your own running history is the most accurate data you have.

## Go to a Running Specialty Store

Not a big box retailer. An actual running store where the staff runs.

They'll watch you walk and jog, ask about your mileage and surface, and put you in a few options. Try them. Run in the store. It takes 20 minutes.

The goal isn't to let them pick your shoe. It's to quickly narrow hundreds of options down to three or four that fit your foot and your running. Then you do the rest.

## What to Check When You Try Them

- A thumb's width of space at the toe (your foot spreads when it lands)
- Heel holds without slipping
- Forefoot isn't cramped
- The ride feels natural when you jog a few steps

If anything feels off in the store, it gets worse after 10 km.

## Online Research Worth Your Time

A few sources are actually useful.

Doctors of Running posts detailed breakdowns of foam compounds and stack heights without brand affiliation. Reddit's r/running has real runners with real mileage if you search a specific shoe and filter for posts with actual experience.

Ignore listicles, "best of 2025" roundups, and anything with affiliate links front and center.

## Use the Return Policy

Most running stores offer a trial period. Run in the shoe two or three times before deciding.

The first run tells you little. By the third run you know if the cushioning works for your weight and mileage, if there are hot spots, and if your foot agrees with the geometry. If something is wrong by run three, return them.

## What "Best" Actually Means

There is no best shoe. There is best for your weight, your surfaces, your mileage, and your footstrike.

I'm 6'2" and 175 lb, almost all road running, high mileage. Cushioning and durability matter most for me. Someone lighter or doing trail running is solving a different problem.

Know what you're asking the shoe to do. Daily trainer for volume. Lighter shoe for tempo. Race shoe for race day. Once you know the job, the research gets a lot easier.

posts.title: How to Make Your Running Shoes Last Longer (And Know When They're Done)
posts.excerpt: Most runners replace their shoes too late — or not at all. Here's how to read the signs of wear, clean them properly, and get more miles out of every pair.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Make Your Running Shoes Last Longer (And Know When They're Done)

I ran through two pairs of shoes before I understood what "worn out" actually meant. I thought worn out meant the upper was falling apart. It's not. By the time the outside looks destroyed, the midsole — the part that actually protects you — has been dead for weeks.

Once I started paying attention to the right signals, my injury rate dropped and my shoes lasted more miles. Here's what I learned.

## Why Shoe Health Matters More Than You Think

Running shoes don't fail dramatically. They fail quietly. The foam in the midsole compresses over time and stops bouncing back the way it did when new. The cushioning and energy return you felt on day one is still there at month two, and then noticeably less by month four, and then gone — but the shoe still looks mostly fine.

Running in dead shoes is like running on worn-down brake pads. The consequence is delayed but real. Knees, hips, and IT bands tend to be the first to notice.

## The 500–800 km Rule (With Caveats)

The standard guidance is to replace running shoes every 500–800 km. That range is wide for a reason: it depends on your weight, the surfaces you run on, your running form, and the specific shoe.

A heavier runner in a minimalist shoe on pavement will hit that limit closer to 500 km. A lighter runner in a maximal trainer on soft trails might get 800 km or more.

Track your mileage. Most running apps do this automatically. If you're not tracking, start now — a rough log is better than nothing.

| Runner Type | Expected Lifespan |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Heavier runner, hard surfaces | 400–550 km |
| Average runner, mixed surfaces | 550–700 km |
| Lighter runner, soft surfaces | 650–800 km |
| Race flats / carbon shoes | 250–400 km |

Carbon-plated race shoes wear out faster than daily trainers. The plate holds up, but the foam around it does not.

## Signs Your Shoes Are Done

Mileage is a useful trigger, but it's not the only one. Here's what to actually look for.

### The Midsole Press Test

Hold the shoe in both hands and press your thumbs into the midsole — the layer of foam between the upper and the outsole. On a fresh shoe, it's noticeably springy. On a worn shoe, it compresses easily with little resistance and doesn't spring back quickly.

Do this comparison on a new pair if you can. The difference is obvious once you've felt it.

### Uneven Wear on the Outsole

Flip the shoe over. Some wear on the ball of the foot and heel is normal. But when the tread pattern is completely smooth in those areas, you've lost the outsole — and the midsole underneath has been absorbing extra impact every step.

### The Twist Test

Hold the heel in one hand and the toe in the other. Try to twist the shoe in opposite directions. A shoe with structural integrity resists this. A worn-out shoe will rotate easily, which means the support structure has broken down.

### You Feel It in Your Body

This one is underrated. If your legs feel more beaten up after easy runs than they used to, and nothing else has changed in your training, your shoes are worth checking first. Aches in the knees, Achilles, or shins that weren't there before are often shoe-related.

## How to Clean Running Shoes Properly

Wet and muddy shoes break down faster if you let them sit. Cleaning regularly extends their life.

### What to Do

- **Remove the insoles** after every run. Let them dry separately.
- **Knock off dried mud** before it sets hard into the material.
- **Rinse with cold water** and a soft brush for the outsole and upper.
- **Use mild soap** if needed — dish soap works fine. Avoid harsh detergents.
- **Air dry at room temperature**, away from direct sunlight and heat sources.

### What Not to Do

- **Don't put them in the washing machine.** The agitation breaks down the midsole foam and the glue holding the upper together.
- **Don't put them in the dryer.** High heat degrades the foam and can warp the shape.
- **Don't dry them in direct sunlight.** UV exposure breaks down materials faster than you'd expect.
- **Don't stuff them with newspaper for drying and leave it for days.** A little newspaper to help shape-dry overnight is fine, but leaving it too long traps moisture.

For mud-caked trail shoes, a garden hose on a gentle setting is your best tool. A toothbrush gets into the lugs.

## How to Store Shoes Between Runs

Storage matters more than most runners think.

Keep shoes somewhere with decent airflow. A mesh bag or open shelf is better than a sealed box or a gym bag left in a hot car. Trapped heat and humidity are the fastest ways to degrade foam outside of actually running in them.

If you run in rain or sweat heavily, loosen the laces and open the tongue after every run so the inside dries fully before your next session.

## Rotate Between Two Pairs

This is the single highest-leverage change I made. Running in the same shoes every day doesn't give the foam time to decompress between sessions.

Foam is viscoelastic — it compresses under load and slowly expands back. If you run 10 km today and then do another run tomorrow, the foam hasn't fully recovered. Rotating between two pairs gives each 24–48 hours of rest, which research suggests meaningfully extends shoe life and may reduce injury risk.

A secondary benefit: you can retire shoes gradually. One pair becomes your easy-day shoe while the newer pair handles harder sessions.

## When to Move to Rotation vs. Replacement

Not every worn pair needs to go in the bin immediately. A shoe that's too worn for hard training can still serve as a walking shoe, a travel shoe, or a recovery-day shoe for short easy efforts.

When the midsole is genuinely dead — no spring, obvious compression — that's full retirement. But a shoe at 80% of its life still has value in a reduced role.

## A Practical Tracking System

You don't need a complicated system. Here's what works:

1. **Log every run with mileage** — Garmin, Strava, or even a notes app.
2. **Tag shoes** if your app supports it (Strava and Garmin both do this automatically).
3. **Set a reminder at 500 km** to do the midsole press test and outsole check.
4. **Start looking for a replacement pair around 500 km** so you're not scrambling if they die.

Strava does this particularly well. You can add each pair of shoes to your gear list, tag them to individual activities, and Strava tracks cumulative mileage per shoe. When a pair is done, you can mark it as retired so it stops accumulating mileage and sits clearly in the "inactive" list. It's a small feature but useful if you're already logging runs there.

For what it's worth, we've looked at building shoe mileage tracking into TrainPace. The functionality isn't complicated. But most runners who want this already have it in Strava or Garmin, and the overlap isn't high enough to make it a priority right now. If that changes, we'll add it.

Buying a new pair before you need them sounds wasteful. Running through a dead pair and getting injured is more expensive.

## The Bottom Line

Shoes are the most important piece of gear a runner owns, and most runners don't pay nearly enough attention to them. You don't need to be precious about it — just do the basics.

Clean them. Dry them properly. Rotate if you can. Track the mileage. Check the foam when you hit 500 km. Replace them before your body tells you to instead of after.

Your knees will notice.

posts.title: How to Pace a Marathon: The Complete Strategy Guide
posts.excerpt: Most marathons are lost in the first 10km, not the last. Here's exactly how to pace from start to finish — including splits, effort, and what to do when it gets hard.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Pace a Marathon: The Complete Strategy Guide

The marathon is the only race where the most dangerous moment is the first one. Go out 10 seconds per kilometer too fast and you'll pay for it from kilometer 30 onward. Get it right, and you'll pass 30 runners in the final 5 kilometers.

This guide is about getting it right.

## The Two Mistakes That Ruin Marathons

### 1. Starting Too Fast

Think about your last race. Did you feel great at the halfway point? How about at 35 km? The gap between those two feelings is usually correlated with how fast you ran the first 10 km.

The crowd is electric at the start. Your legs feel fresh. Your training is done. Everything says: go. And if you go, you'll pay.

The glycogen depletion that causes the "wall" at 30–35 km is accelerated by early pace mistakes. Running 15 seconds per kilometer too fast in the first half burns through your glycogen reserves faster and leaves you running on empty exactly when you need to dig deepest.

### 2. Running by Feel Instead of Target Pace

In the first 10 km of a marathon, your correct target pace feels easy. Genuinely, disconcertingly easy. Runners who haven't experienced this will ignore the GPS watch and run by feel — which produces a pace that is too fast.

This is why you need a plan before race day.

## Even Split vs. Negative Split

The two standard approaches to marathon pacing are:

**Even split**: Same pace per kilometer for the entire 42.195 km. This requires enormous discipline in the first half and tends to produce the most consistent results for recreational runners.

**Negative split**: Run the second half faster than the first. The most elite marathon times are run with modest negative splits (1–2 minutes faster in the second half). For recreational runners, even targeting a flat-or-slightly-faster approach often results in a natural negative split given early-race conservatism.

For most runners, **targeting even splits and running a small negative split** is the optimal strategy.

## How to Calculate Your Target Pace

Start with your goal finish time. Use the [pace calculator](/calculator) to find your exact kilometer or mile split.

For a 3:30:00 marathon:
- Target pace: 4:58/km (7:59/mile)
- First half: 1:45:00
- Second half: 1:44:30–1:45:00 (flat or slightly faster)

Print this split on your wrist, set your watch to pace alerts, and stick to it for the first 25 km regardless of how you feel.

## The Boston Qualifier Rule of Thumb

For runners targeting significant PRs, there's a simple rule: **if you feel comfortable, you're running correctly**. If you feel good at 20 km, something is wrong. If you feel in control — working but controlled — you're on target.

The discomfort starts around 28–32 km for most runners. If it starts earlier, you went out too fast. If it never comes, you had more left.

## Splits by Phase

### Kilometers 1–10: Conservative

Your body is warming up. Glycogen is full. Legs feel springy. This is when the race feels easiest and when the most damage is done.

Run 5–10 seconds per kilometer **slower** than target pace. If your goal is 5:00/km, run 5:05–5:10. The time you lose here is recovered in the back half when others fade.

### Kilometers 10–25: Settle In

This is your cruise phase. Find your rhythm. Hit target pace consistently. Don't react to runners passing you or the crowd. Execute the plan.

### Kilometers 25–35: The Test

This is where the race begins. Your body is running low on glycogen. The legs are getting heavy. Every kilometer is a negotiation.

Focus shifts from pace to effort. Maintain the same **effort level** you've had since kilometer 10. Pace may slow slightly — that's acceptable. What matters is keeping the effort honest.

This is also when fueling mistakes compound. If you've been under-fueling all race, you'll know by kilometer 30.

### Kilometers 35–42.195: Everything You Have

Pace doesn't matter now. All that matters is forward motion. Shorten your stride if your form is breaking down. Take the tangents. Count to 100. Focus on the next runner. Do whatever you need to reach the finish line.

If you've paced it correctly, your final 7 km will be among the fastest you've run all day.

## Weather Adjustments

Hot and humid conditions require a significant pace adjustment. A rule of thumb:
- Over 16°C: add 5–10 seconds/km
- Over 20°C: add 15–20 seconds/km
- Over 24°C: add 30+ seconds/km or adjust your expectations significantly

Ignoring weather is a common cause of late-race blowups.

## The Fueling Connection

Pacing and fueling are inseparable. A perfect pacing strategy can unravel without adequate carbohydrate intake. For most marathons:

- Start fueling at kilometer 5 (earlier than you think you need to)
- Target 40–60g of carbohydrates per hour
- Take gels or chews at regular intervals, not when you feel like you need them (by then it's too late)

Use the [Fuel Planner](/fuel) to calculate exactly how many gels to carry, when to take them, and which aid stations to target.

## Race Day Checklist

**The night before:**
- Know your target pace per km and total split time
- Load your GPS watch with pace alerts
- Confirm your fueling plan (number of gels, timing)

**Race morning:**
- Don't start faster than target pace, even if you feel great
- Run the tangents (stay on the inside of curves) — saves 100–200 meters
- Start hydrating before you're thirsty

**When it gets hard (it will):**
- Shorten your stride before you slow your cadence
- Focus on maintaining form, not pace
- Remember: everyone is suffering at kilometer 35

## Using the Pace Calculator Before Race Day

The best pre-race tool I know is the [pace calculator](/calculator). Enter your goal time, get your target pace per kilometer, and print it on your wrist.

If you have a recent race time (5K, 10K, half marathon), you can also use the VDOT-based race prediction to check whether your marathon goal is realistic. An overambitious goal time is the most common cause of a blown marathon.

[Calculate your marathon splits →](/calculator)

posts.title: What is VDOT? The Running Metric That Changes How You Train
posts.excerpt: VDOT is a single number that describes your current running fitness — and unlocks scientifically correct training paces for every zone from easy to interval. Here's how it works and why it matters.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# What is VDOT? The Running Metric That Changes How You Train

If you've ever wondered why one workout leaves you feeling strong and another leaves you broken — even though they look similar on paper — the answer is usually pacing. Specifically, running at the wrong intensity for that session's purpose.

VDOT fixes this. It gives you a single number that describes your current running fitness, then derives exact training paces for five distinct zones from that number. No more guessing. No more running your "easy" run at half-marathon effort and wondering why you feel wrecked on Thursday.

## Where VDOT Comes From

VDOT is a concept from exercise scientist Jack Daniels, whose book *Daniels' Running Formula* has shaped modern distance training methodology. (The capitalization is intentional — it's not the same as VO2max, though the two are related.)

VO2max is your body's maximum capacity to consume oxygen — a laboratory measurement that requires a treadmill test and a mask. Most runners never get a formal VO2max test.

VDOT sidesteps the lab. It's a calculated value derived from a race result: your time plus the distance produces a number that reflects your *effective* aerobic capacity — what you're actually able to do in a race, accounting for running economy and all the other factors that separate theoretical VO2max from real-world performance.

A 50-year-old who trains consistently might have a lower VO2max than a 25-year-old but outperform them in a race due to better economy, pacing, and mental toughness. VDOT captures this. It measures what you do, not what your physiology suggests you should be able to do.

## How to Calculate Your VDOT

All you need is a recent race result — any distance from 1500m to the marathon.

Enter your time and distance into the [VDOT Calculator](/vdot). It computes your VDOT score and immediately shows:

- Your **training paces** for each zone (Easy, Marathon, Threshold, Interval, Repetition)
- Your **race predictions** across all common distances
- A **percentile ranking** among runners globally
- **Sample workouts** for each training zone

The calculation uses Daniels' oxygen cost model, which relates running velocity to oxygen consumption via an empirically validated equation.

## The Five Training Zones

VDOT unlocks five distinct training zones, each with a specific physiological purpose:

### Easy Pace (E)
The foundation zone. Conversational, relaxed, aerobically stimulating without creating meaningful stress. Most of your weekly mileage should be here — 70-80% for most runners.

*Purpose:* Build aerobic base, capillary density, and mitochondrial volume. Aid recovery. Build running economy.

*Feel:* You can hold a full conversation. Your breathing is rhythmic and relaxed.

### Marathon Pace (M)
The pace you'd sustain for a full marathon — controlled effort, not comfortable.

*Purpose:* Train fat oxidation at race-specific intensity. Prepare legs and mind for marathon effort.

*Feel:* Controlled but purposeful. You can speak in sentences but don't want to.

### Threshold Pace (T)
Also called "tempo pace" — the fastest pace you can sustain for about an hour. This is where lactate accumulates as fast as your body clears it.

*Purpose:* Raise the speed at which you can run aerobically. Directly translates to race fitness at all distances.

*Feel:* "Comfortably uncomfortable." You can say a few words but not full sentences.

### Interval Pace (I)
VO2max training. Runs at your 3K-5K race effort, sustained for 3-5 minutes at a time with equal recovery.

*Purpose:* Increase maximum oxygen uptake. Push the ceiling on aerobic capacity.

*Feel:* Hard. You can say one or two words. Heart rate is very high.

### Repetition Pace (R)
Short, fast efforts at your mile race pace or faster, with full recovery between.

*Purpose:* Improve speed and running economy. Train neuromuscular efficiency.

*Feel:* Very hard but short — 30 seconds to 2 minutes per rep, full rest.

## Why These Paces Matter More Than Effort

Effort is subjective. A 5/10 effort on a hot, humid Tuesday after a poor night of sleep is physiologically different from 5/10 on a cool Saturday morning rested. Running by feel without an objective anchor leads to drifting — easy runs get too hard, hard runs don't get hard enough.

VDOT paces are calibrated to what your body can actually do right now, derived from real race data. They account for your current fitness — not the fitness you had three months ago, not the fitness you want to have, but what you proved in a recent race.

This is especially important for easy runs. The VDOT easy pace often surprises runners because it feels *too* slow. That feeling is the adaptation mechanism working. The stress-response cycle of easy aerobic running happens below the lactate threshold — running harder doesn't accelerate the adaptations, it just generates more fatigue.

## How VDOT Changes As You Improve

VDOT isn't fixed. As you train, race, and adapt, it rises. A VDOT of 40 in January might become 44 by June if you've trained consistently and raced.

When your VDOT goes up, all your training paces shift upward with it. This is why re-testing — running a tune-up race every 6-8 weeks — matters. Training at outdated paces means either running too slow (missing stimulus) or running too hard (accumulating unnecessary fatigue).

The [VDOT What-If Explorer](/vdot) lets you slide the VDOT value and see what training paces and race predictions would look like at a higher score — useful for setting realistic targets.

## VDOT vs. Pace-Based Training

Some coaches prescribe training zones based on pace alone — run your easy runs at X:XX/km, your tempo at Y:YY/km. This works if those paces are calibrated to your fitness. The problem is they usually aren't, or they aren't updated when fitness changes.

VDOT solves both problems: the initial calibration comes from a real race, and updating it is as simple as entering a new race result.

The two approaches converge when done correctly. VDOT is just a principled method for deriving correct paces from objective data.

## Practical First Steps

1. **Run a race** — even a 5K is enough. Time trials work if you haven't raced recently, though official race results are more reliable since external pressure produces a more accurate effort.

2. **Enter it into the VDOT Calculator** — [trainpace.com/vdot](/vdot). Your score and training paces appear immediately.

3. **Note your Easy and Threshold paces** — these will anchor 90% of your training.

4. **Build a plan around those paces** — the [Training Plan Generator](/plan) will accept your VDOT-derived paces directly if you navigate from the VDOT results page.

5. **Retest in 6-8 weeks** — run another race or a structured time trial. Recalibrate.

The first time you look at your VDOT easy pace and realize you've been running easy days 90 seconds per kilometer too fast, everything clicks. The training starts making sense. The improvement follows.

[Calculate your VDOT →](/vdot)

posts.title: I Ran the Same Time as Last Year — And It Was a Completely Different Race
posts.excerpt: BMO Vancouver Half 2026 race report. 2:06 twice, two completely different runners. Why the clock is lying — and what the heat cost me.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# I Ran the Same Time as Last Year — And It Was a Completely Different Race

**BMO Vancouver Half Marathon 2026 | Race Report**

---

Last year I ran the BMO Vancouver Half Marathon in 2:06:27. This year I ran it in 2:06:08. Same course. Same distance. Same number on the clock.

Completely different race.

## The Goal

Sub-2:00. I'd spent 12 weeks in a structured training block with my coach, built a pacing strategy, dialled in a fueling plan, and came in with a 55:00 10K on tired legs two weeks prior. The fitness was there. Every predictor said 1:55–1:58 was realistic.

Then race day was 15°C at the gun, climbing toward 20°C by the back half.

## The Plan

The strategy was a controlled negative split:

- **Km 1–3:** 5:45/km — resist the downhill, save the quads
- **Km 4–8:** 5:40/km — settle in
- **Km 9–21.1:** 5:35/km — build and hold

Four gels: km 3, km 7, a caffeinated gel at km 12, and a final one at km 15–16 to cover the exact stretch where I blew up last year. Water at every station, poured over the head to keep core temperature managed.

## What Happened

The first few kilometres went mostly to plan. 5:37 on km 1, 5:42 on km 2. I surged to 5:20 on km 3 — not because I lost discipline, but because the start corral was packed with runners pacing 7:00–7:30. I had to get clear or spend the next 5 km weaving through traffic on a steep downhill descent.

Through km 14, I was steady, averaging 5:40–5:50. Heart rate in check. Power output holding around 220–245w. Then the heat started to take its toll.

Km 15: 6:19. Km 16: 6:06. The drift was real, and I could feel it — legs heavy, not painful, just unresponsive. I got annoyed and surged back to 5:41 at km 17. But the heat cashed that check at km 20 — 6:47.

Finished in 2:06:08.

## Last Year vs. This Year — Same Time, Different Story

Here's why the clock is lying.

**Last year (7°C, perfect conditions):**
- Went out at 5:31/km on the descent
- HR hit 177 by km 19
- Power collapsed to 184w
- Km 19: 7:03. Km 20: 7:20. Km 21: 7:06
- Could barely walk after

**This year (15–20°C):**
- Started at 5:37/km — controlled
- HR stayed in check around 160–173, partly thanks to water on the head
- Power never dropped below 199w
- Worst km was 6:47 — once
- Walked away fine

Last year I bonked. My heart rate was maxed and my muscles had nothing left. This year I faded in the heat but maintained composure, fueled properly, and finished with something left in the tank that the temperature simply wouldn't let me use.

## The 10K Comparison That Tells the Real Story

Two weeks before this race, I ran a 10K in 55:00 — on legs that had done an 18 km easy run the day before. Controlled negative split, kicked 4:45 on the last km.

Last April, one week before the 2:06 half, I ran a 10K in 55:31 on fresh legs. Started at 4:55 and faded to 5:59 twice. Erratic, white-knuckled effort.

Same time. Completely different execution. The training block changed how I race, even if the half marathon clock didn't show it.

## What the Heat Costs

Research suggests that for every degree above roughly 12°C, performance degrades by about 1–1.5% for trained runners. At 20°C, that's a 3–4% hit — which on a 1:56 target pace translates to 4–5 minutes.

1:56 + 5 minutes = 2:01. Add corral congestion and some hill fade? 2:06 makes perfect sense.

On the course, I watched runners collapse and get attended to by medical staff. The heat wasn't a minor inconvenience — it was the defining variable of the race. Staying upright, finishing strong enough to walk after, and not ending up on a stretcher was the real win, even if it doesn't feel like one.

## What I'm Taking Forward

The sub-2 is there. The data confirms it. The pacing discipline was there. The fueling was there. The mental toughness was there — I surged back from a drift at km 17 instead of letting the race unravel.

What I need is either cooler weather or a bigger engine. I'm choosing the bigger engine. My plan for the summer is to stop taking seasons off, ramp weekly volume from 20 km to 40–50 km, and build the kind of aerobic base that makes sub-2 a formality in any conditions.

The goal isn't to become a runner who can break 2:00 on the right day. It's to become a runner where 2:00 is never in question.

2:06 twice. Same number. Different runner. The next one will be different on the clock too.

posts.title: Understanding VDOT and Training Paces: A Complete Guide
posts.excerpt: Learn how VDOT works, why it matters for your training, and how to use pace zones effectively. This is the system that helped me drop 55 minutes off my half marathon.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Understanding VDOT and Training Paces

When I ran my first half marathon at BMO Vancouver in 2024, I finished in 3:01:00. I had no idea what pace I should be running in training. I just ran as hard as I could, every single day.

A year later, I crossed the same finish line in 2:06:00—55 minutes faster. The difference wasn't just more miles. It was running the *right* pace on the *right* days. And that started with understanding VDOT.

## What is VDOT?

VDOT is a measure of your current running fitness, developed by legendary coach Dr. Jack Daniels. Unlike VO2max (which requires lab testing), VDOT can be estimated from any recent race performance.

The beauty of VDOT is that it gives you a single number that unlocks all your training paces:

- **Easy pace** for recovery runs
- **Marathon pace** for long run segments
- **Threshold pace** for tempo runs
- **Interval pace** for track workouts
- **Repetition pace** for speed development

## Why This Changed Everything for Me

Before I understood pace zones, I ran every run at the same moderate effort. It felt productive, but I was actually sabotaging my training.

When I joined a run club in Vancouver, they forced me to slow down on easy days. I'm talking runs that felt almost embarrassingly slow. But here's what happened:

1. I stopped feeling constantly exhausted
2. My speed workouts actually felt fast
3. I could string together consistent weeks without breaking down
4. I stopped obsessing over my watch during runs

The breakthrough wasn't running harder. It was **running smarter**.

## How to Find Your VDOT

The easiest way is to use a [pace calculator](/calculator) with a recent race result. Enter your time from a 5K, 10K, half marathon, or marathon, and you'll get your VDOT score instantly.

I built the TrainPace calculator because I got frustrated with other tools—buried in ads, clunky unit conversions, hidden features. I just wanted my zones without dodging clickbait.

### Example VDOT Paces

For a runner with a 45:00 10K (VDOT ~45):

| Zone | Pace/Mile | Purpose |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Easy | 9:30-10:00 | Recovery, base building |
| Marathon | 8:15 | Race-specific endurance |
| Threshold | 7:35 | Lactate clearance |
| Interval | 7:00 | VO2max development |
| Repetition | 6:30 | Running economy |

## The Hardest Lesson: Easy Days Are Easy

Most runners—myself included—run their easy days too fast. You feel good, so you push the pace. But this leads to:

- Incomplete recovery between hard sessions
- Diminished quality on workout days
- Plateauing or declining performance

My run club taught me that easy pace should be 2 minutes slower than race pace. When I first tried this, it felt ridiculous. But that's where the adaptation happens.

## Adjusting Your VDOT

Your VDOT should increase as you get fitter. Re-test every 4-6 weeks with a time trial or race. As your VDOT rises, your training paces will adjust accordingly.

I went from barely finishing a half marathon to targeting sub-2:00. Every feature in TrainPace is something I used to plan, execute, and improve my own racing.

## Getting Started

Ready to find your training paces? Head to our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) and enter your most recent race time. You'll have personalized paces in seconds.

Remember: the goal isn't to run fast every day. It's to run the *right* pace every day. That's how a 3:01 becomes a 2:06.

posts.title: Marathon Fueling: Everything You Need to Know
posts.excerpt: From carb loading to race-day nutrition, here's how to fuel properly. I learned this the hard way—10 gels and 2 clunky bottles my first race.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Marathon Fueling: Everything You Need to Know

My first half marathon at BMO Vancouver, I brought 10 gels and 2 clunky water bottles. I had no idea what I actually needed. I just wanted to prove I could do something hard.

I finished in 3:01:00 and spent the next week barely able to walk down stairs.

A year later, I used the fuel planner I'd built, and it told me I needed 3 gels. I didn't fully trust it, so I brought 5. Turns out, 3 would've been enough. Even I didn't believe my own math at first.

That's why I'm writing this—so you don't make the same mistakes I did.

## Why Fueling Matters

Your body stores about 2,000 calories of glycogen (stored carbs) in your muscles and liver. A marathon burns approximately 2,600-3,000 calories. A half marathon burns about 1,300-1,500.

Without taking in fuel during the race, you *will* run out of glycogen. When that happens, you "bonk" or "hit the wall"—a sudden, dramatic loss of energy that can turn a great race into a survival march.

## Pre-Race Nutrition

### Carb Loading (2-3 Days Before)

Carb loading isn't about eating pasta until you're sick. It's about:

- Increasing carb intake to 8-10g per kg of body weight
- Reducing fiber to avoid GI issues
- Staying well-hydrated

**Good carb loading foods:**
- White rice and pasta
- Bread, bagels, English muffins
- Potatoes (without skin)
- Bananas
- Sports drinks

### Race Morning (2-4 Hours Before)

Eat a familiar breakfast with 100-150g of carbs:

- Oatmeal with banana
- Bagel with peanut butter
- Toast with jam

Avoid high-fiber, high-fat foods that could cause stomach issues.

## During the Race

### The Golden Rules

1. **Start early** - Begin fueling by mile 4-5, not when you're already hungry
2. **Stay consistent** - Take fuel every 20-30 minutes
3. **Practice in training** - Never try anything new on race day

### How Much to Take

Aim for 30-60g of carbs per hour. Most gels contain 20-25g, so that's:

- 1 gel every 20-30 minutes, OR
- Equivalent from sports drink, chews, or real food

### Gel vs. Chews vs. Drink

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Gels | Fast absorption, portable | Can cause GI distress |
| Chews | Easier on stomach, satisfying | Need to chew while running |
| Sports drink | Combines fuel + hydration | Heavy to carry |

## Creating Your Fueling Plan

I built the [Fuel Planner](/fuel) because fueling calculators didn't exist when I was training. How many gels do I actually need? I had to do the math myself.

Now you can create a personalized race-day nutrition strategy based on:

- Your target pace
- Race distance
- Aid station locations
- Preferred fuel sources

## Common Mistakes

1. **Bringing too much** - Like me with 10 gels. Calculate what you need.
2. **Skipping breakfast** - You need those pre-race carbs
3. **Starting fuel too late** - By the time you're hungry, it's too late
4. **Trying new products** - Test everything in training
5. **Ignoring electrolytes** - Sodium loss affects performance

## What I Do Now

For my 2:06 half marathon, I had a plan:
- Gel at km 7
- Gel at km 14
- Water at every aid station

Simple. Practiced. Executed.

No bonking. No regrets. I crossed the line smiling instead of limping.

## The Bottom Line

Proper fueling won't make you faster than your fitness allows, but improper fueling will definitely make you slower. Plan ahead, practice in training, and execute on race day.

Ready to create your plan? Try our [Fuel Planner](/fuel) to dial in your nutrition strategy. Don't be the person with 10 gels and 2 bottles wondering what went wrong.

posts.title: How Elevation Affects Your Race Strategy
posts.excerpt: Hills can make or break your race. I analyzed the BMO course km by km before my PR—here's how to do the same for yours.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How Elevation Affects Your Race Strategy

Before my second BMO Vancouver Half Marathon, I analyzed the course elevation profile km by km. I knew where the hills were, how steep they'd be, and exactly how I'd adjust my effort.

That 55-minute drop wasn't luck. Part of it was knowing the terrain before I got there.

## The Cost of Climbing

Running uphill requires significantly more energy than running on flat ground. Research suggests:

- **4% grade**: ~15% more energy expenditure
- **8% grade**: ~30% more energy expenditure
- **Downhills**: Save some energy, but stress muscles differently

This means a hilly race is effectively "longer" than a flat one in terms of effort.

## Why I Built the Elevation Finder

Existing elevation analysis tools frustrated me. I wanted to see grade percentages and plan my effort, not just stare at a static chart. Strava hides useful features. Other sites are buried in ads.

So I built something that actually helps you understand your course.

## Reading Elevation Profiles

When analyzing a course, look for:

### Total Elevation Gain

The cumulative feet/meters climbed. Boston has ~800ft of gain, while Chicago has only ~200ft. That matters.

### Elevation Placement

Where the hills fall matters:

- **Early hills** (miles 1-10): Easier to manage with fresh legs
- **Mid-race hills** (miles 10-20): Can disrupt rhythm
- **Late hills** (miles 20+): Most challenging when glycogen depleted

### Grade Severity

Short, steep hills hit differently than long, gradual inclines. Know what you're facing.

## Pacing Strategies for Hills

### The Effort-Based Approach

Instead of maintaining pace, maintain effort:

- **Uphills**: Slow down, keep heart rate steady
- **Downhills**: Let gravity help, but don't brake excessively
- **Flats**: Return to goal pace

This is what I did at BMO. Controlled effort on the hills. Disciplined pacing in the early kilometers. No panic when my watch showed slower splits on inclines.

### Time Cost Estimates

Rough guidelines for pace adjustment:

| Grade | Pace Adjustment |
|-------|----------------|
| 2% up | +15 sec/mile |
| 4% up | +30 sec/mile |
| 2% down | -10 sec/mile |
| 4% down | -15 sec/mile |

## Analyzing Your Race Course

Use our [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder) to:

1. Upload your course GPX file
2. View the elevation profile
3. Identify key climbs and descents
4. Plan your segment-by-segment strategy

## Training for Hills

If your goal race is hilly, your training should include:

- **Hill repeats**: Build strength and climbing efficiency
- **Long runs with elevation**: Simulate race conditions
- **Downhill practice**: Protect your quads and practice controlled descents

## Major Marathon Elevation Profiles

![elevation-chart](boston "Boston Marathon — the famous Newton Hills in miles 16–21")

![route-map](boston "Boston Marathon course: Hopkinton to Boylston Street")


We have detailed breakdowns for major marathons:

- [Boston Marathon](/preview-route/boston) - The famous Newton Hills
- [New York City Marathon](/preview-route/nyc) - Five bridges, rolling terrain
- [Chicago Marathon](/preview-route/chicago) - Pan flat and fast
- [Berlin Marathon](/preview-route/berlin) - Flat PR course

## The Mental Game

Hills are as much mental as physical. Strategies that help:

- **Break hills into segments** - Focus on reaching the next landmark
- **Use mantras** - "Smooth and steady" or "Relax and float"
- **Look ahead, not down** - Keep your posture upright
- **Embrace the challenge** - Hills are where races are won

## Key Takeaways

1. Respect the elevation—it will cost you time and energy
2. Adjust pace by effort, not just by numbers
3. Know your course before race day
4. Train specifically for the terrain you'll face

Upload your race course to our [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder) and start planning your strategy today. Knowing the course is half the battle.

posts.title: First Half or Marathon? What I Wish I Knew
posts.excerpt: I went from not being able to run 6 laps in gym class to finishing a half marathon. Here's what I learned the hard way.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# First Half or Marathon? What I Wish I Knew

As a kid, I couldn't run 6 laps under 15 minutes in gym class. Running wasn't something I did.

Then in May 2024, I crossed the finish line of the BMO Vancouver Half Marathon in 3:01:00. I survived, but barely. The week that followed was brutal—sore, in pain, unable to walk down stairs without wincing.

But finishing gave me something I didn't know I needed: proof that I could push through hard things.

Here's what I wish someone had told me before that first race.

## 1. The Long Run Is King

Your weekly long run is the most important workout of the week. It builds:

- Endurance
- Mental toughness
- Confidence
- Fat-burning efficiency

Don't skip long runs. Don't rush them. Run them at conversational pace.

## 2. Easy Days Must Be Easy

This was the hardest lesson. When I joined a run club, they forced me to slow down on easy days—runs that felt almost embarrassingly slow at the time.

But that's where the adaptation happened.

Easy pace should be about 2 minutes slower than race pace. If you can't hold a full conversation, slow down.

Use our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to find your proper easy pace.

## 3. Consistency Beats Intensity

Four 40-minute runs per week beats one 3-hour hero session. Your body adapts to regular stress, not occasional big efforts.

I stayed consistent for 24 weeks before my second half. That consistency—not any single workout—is what dropped 55 minutes off my time.

## 4. Rest Is Training

Adaptation happens during recovery, not during workouts. Sleep 7-9 hours. Take rest days seriously. Your body will thank you on race day.

## 5. Nothing New on Race Day

This golden rule applies to:

- Shoes
- Clothing
- Fuel (gels, drinks)
- Breakfast
- Pre-race routine

I brought 10 gels and 2 clunky bottles to my first race because I had no idea what I actually needed. Test everything in training.

## 6. Start Slower Than You Think

The first few miles will feel easy. You'll be tempted to bank time. Don't.

Start 10-15 seconds slower than goal pace. You can always speed up later. You can't get those early miles back.

## 7. Fuel Early and Often

Don't wait until you're hungry to eat. By then, it's too late.

Start taking gels/fuel by km 6-8 and continue every 20-30 minutes. Check out our [Fuel Planner](/fuel) to create your strategy.

## 8. Expect the Hard Part (But Plan Around It)

Km 15-18 in a half marathon will be hard. Really hard. This is normal. It happens to everyone.

Having a plan helps:
- Break the race into segments
- Have mantras ready
- Know that it will pass

## 9. Enjoy the Experience

Your first long race only happens once. Look around. Read the signs. High-five the kids. This is a celebration.

## 10. Finishing Is the Goal

Forget time goals for your first race. Seriously.

The goal is to cross the finish line healthy and happy. Everything else is a bonus.

A year after my 3:01, I crossed the line in 2:06—smiling instead of limping. But that first finish, as painful as it was, proved to my inner child that hard things are possible.

---

Ready to start? Get your [training paces](/calculator), analyze your [race course](/elevation-finder), and plan your [fueling strategy](/fuel).

You've got this.

posts.title: Recovery Strategies That Actually Work
posts.excerpt: I dealt with nagging knee pain from skipping strength training. Here's what I learned about recovery the hard way.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Recovery Strategies That Actually Work

During my 24-week training block for BMO Vancouver, I dealt with nagging knee pain. Probably from skipping strength training—a mistake I won't make again.

Training breaks you down. Recovery builds you back up stronger. Here's what I learned about recovering smarter.

## Why Recovery Matters

Adaptation happens during rest, not during workouts. When you run:

- Muscle fibers tear (micro-damage)
- Glycogen depletes
- Inflammation increases
- Hormones fluctuate

Recovery reverses this damage and builds you back stronger. Skip it, and you'll plateau—or worse, get injured like I did.

## The Big Three: Sleep, Nutrition, Easy Days

### 1. Sleep (The #1 Recovery Tool)

No supplement, gadget, or ice bath comes close to sleep:

- **7-9 hours** is the target for most runners
- **Growth hormone** peaks during deep sleep
- **Memory consolidation** helps motor learning
- **Immune function** repairs during rest

**Sleep tips for runners:**
- Keep a consistent schedule
- Cool, dark room (65-68°F / 18-20°C)
- No screens 1 hour before bed
- Avoid caffeine after 2pm

### 2. Post-Run Nutrition

The 30-60 minute window after running is prime time for recovery:

- **Protein**: 20-30g to repair muscle (whey, Greek yogurt, eggs)
- **Carbs**: Replenish glycogen (banana, rice, oats)
- **Ratio**: 3:1 or 4:1 carbs to protein

**Quick recovery snacks:**
- Chocolate milk (seriously, it works)
- Banana + peanut butter
- Greek yogurt + granola
- Protein smoothie

### 3. Easy Days Are Easy

This is where most runners fail. I know—I was one of them.

My run club in Vancouver forced me to slow down. Easy runs should be:

- **Conversational pace** - You can talk in full sentences
- **About 2 minutes slower** than race pace
- **Low heart rate** - Zone 2 training

Use our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to find your proper easy pace. Running too fast on easy days is the #1 cause of overtraining.

## Active Recovery Methods

### Walking

The most underrated recovery tool:
- Promotes blood flow without stress
- 20-30 minutes on rest days
- Great for mental recovery too

### Foam Rolling

Research shows moderate benefits for:
- Reducing muscle soreness
- Improving short-term flexibility
- **Best timing**: Post-run or before bed

### Stretching

- **Dynamic stretching**: Before runs
- **Static stretching**: After runs or separate sessions
- Hold stretches 30-60 seconds

## Recovery Week (Deload)

Every 3-4 weeks, reduce training volume by 20-30%:

- Fewer miles/kilometers
- Same intensity on key workouts
- More sleep
- Focus on form, not fitness

This prevents overtraining and allows deeper adaptation.

## Warning Signs of Under-Recovery

Watch for these red flags:

- Persistent fatigue
- Elevated resting heart rate
- Poor sleep quality
- Irritability, mood changes
- Decreased performance
- Frequent illness
- Loss of motivation

If you notice several of these, take extra rest days. I wish I'd listened to these signals earlier with my knee.

## The Bottom Line

Recovery isn't lazy—it's strategic. The runners who recover best can train the hardest. Prioritize sleep, eat well, and respect easy days.

Your next PR is built on rest days. Trust me—I learned this one the hard way.

posts.title: Tempo Runs: The Workout That Made Me Faster
posts.excerpt: One speed workout per week was part of my 24-week plan. Here's how tempo runs work and why they're non-negotiable.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Tempo Runs: The Workout That Made Me Faster

My 24-week training plan had one simple structure:
- 1 long run per week
- 1 speed workout per week
- Easy runs 2 minutes slower than race pace

That speed workout was usually a tempo run. Here's why they work and how to do them right.

## What Is a Tempo Run?

A tempo run is a sustained effort at "comfortably hard" pace—faster than easy, slower than racing. The goal is to run at or near your **lactate threshold**: the pace where lactate production and clearance are balanced.

**The feeling**: You can speak in short phrases, but not full sentences. It's challenging but sustainable.

## Why Tempo Runs Work

At threshold pace, you're training your body to:

1. **Clear lactate more efficiently** - Push back the point where you "bonk"
2. **Improve running economy** - Become more efficient at faster paces
3. **Build mental toughness** - Practice holding discomfort
4. **Race-specific fitness** - Half marathon pace is close to threshold

## Finding Your Tempo Pace

There are several ways to find it:

### Method 1: Race Prediction

Use our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) with a recent race time. Your threshold pace is typically:

- About 25-30 seconds per km slower than 10K pace
- About 15-20 seconds per km faster than half marathon pace

### Method 2: Heart Rate

85-90% of max heart rate.

### Method 3: Perceived Effort

6-7 out of 10 effort. "Comfortably hard." You want to slow down but don't need to.

## Tempo Run Formats

### Classic Tempo (20-40 minutes)

The standard approach:
- 10-15 min easy warmup
- 20-40 min at tempo pace
- 10 min easy cooldown

**Best for**: General threshold development, half marathon training

### Cruise Intervals

Broken into segments with short recovery:
- 3-4 x 10 minutes at tempo with 2 min jog
- 5 x 6 minutes at tempo with 90 sec jog

**Best for**: When starting tempo training, longer threshold work

### Progression Tempo

Start slower, finish faster:
- First third at marathon pace
- Second third at tempo pace
- Final third at 10K effort

**Best for**: Learning to close races strong

## Common Tempo Mistakes

### 1. Going Too Fast

The most common error. If you're racing the tempo, you're missing the point. It should feel controlled.

### 2. Going Too Long

More isn't always better. 40 minutes of quality tempo beats 60 minutes of sloppy threshold running.

### 3. Skipping Warmup

You need 10-15 minutes of easy running before hitting tempo pace. Cold muscles don't respond well.

## Sample Week with Tempo

| Day | Workout |
|-----|----------|
| Monday | Rest or cross-train |
| Tuesday | Easy run |
| Wednesday | **Tempo: 15 min warmup + 25 min tempo + 10 min cooldown** |
| Thursday | Easy run |
| Friday | Rest |
| Saturday | Long run |
| Sunday | Easy run |

This is close to what I did for 24 weeks. Simple, sustainable, effective.

## The Mental Game

Tempo runs teach you to be comfortable with discomfort. This translates directly to racing:

- You learn what "sustainable hard" feels like
- You practice not panicking when it gets tough
- You build confidence in your fitness

By race day, I knew exactly what tempo felt like. I trusted my legs because I'd done the work.

## Key Takeaways

1. Tempo = threshold pace = comfortably hard
2. Use the [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to find your tempo pace
3. Start with 20 minutes and build gradually
4. Don't race your tempo runs—control is key
5. One tempo per week is plenty for most runners

Consistent tempo work will make you a faster, more confident racer. Trust the process.

posts.title: How to Choose the Right Running Shoes
posts.excerpt: Cut through the marketing hype. Here's what actually matters when selecting running shoes—from someone who's logged plenty of kilometers in Vancouver rain.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Choose the Right Running Shoes

With hundreds of options and endless marketing claims, choosing running shoes can be overwhelming. Here's what actually matters.

For context: I'm 6'2" and 175 lb, almost all road running, and I run high mileage. Cushioning and durability matter a lot to me. Trail runners or lighter runners will have different priorities.

## The Truth About Running Shoes

Let's start with what research tells us:

1. **Comfort is king** - The best predictor of injury prevention is comfort
2. **Expensive isn't always better** - Price doesn't correlate with injury rates
3. **Pronation control is overrated** - Motion control shoes don't prevent injuries for most runners
4. **Rotation helps** - Using multiple shoes reduces injury risk

## Key Factors to Consider

### 1. Fit

The most important factor:

- **Toe box**: Enough room to wiggle toes
- **Heel**: Snug but not tight, no slipping
- **Width**: Available in multiple widths if needed
- **Length**: Thumb's width between longest toe and front

**Pro tip**: Shop in the afternoon when feet are slightly swollen.

### 2. Cushioning (Stack Height)

How much foam between you and the ground:

| Level | Stack Height | Best For |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| Minimal | 0-15mm | Experienced runners, speed work |
| Low | 15-25mm | Tempo runs, shorter races |
| Medium | 25-35mm | Daily training, versatile |
| Max | 35mm+ | Long runs, recovery, high mileage |

### 3. Drop (Heel-to-Toe Offset)

The height difference between heel and forefoot:

- **High drop (10-12mm)**: Traditional, good for heel strikers
- **Medium drop (6-8mm)**: Balanced, versatile
- **Low drop (0-4mm)**: Promotes midfoot strike, requires adaptation

**Warning**: Don't suddenly switch to low drop shoes. Transition gradually.

### 4. Weight

- **Daily trainers**: 255-310g (9-11 oz)
- **Tempo/race shoes**: 200-255g (7-9 oz)
- **Super shoes (carbon plate)**: 170-230g (6-8 oz)

Lighter shoes are faster but offer less protection.

## Types of Running Shoes

### Daily Trainers

Your workhorse shoe for most miles:
- Durable
- Comfortable
- Moderate cushioning
- 500-800 km lifespan

**Examples**: Nike Pegasus, Brooks Ghost, ASICS Cumulus, New Balance 880

### Tempo/Uptempo Shoes

For faster workouts and shorter races:
- Lighter weight
- More responsive
- Less cushioning
- 300-600 km lifespan

### Racing Flats/Super Shoes

For races and key workouts:
- Carbon fiber plates
- Maximum energy return
- Lightweight
- 150-300 km lifespan

## Building a Shoe Rotation

Research shows rotating shoes reduces injury risk by ~39%. A good rotation:

1. **Daily trainer** (60% of miles)
2. **Uptempo shoe** (25% of miles)
3. **Easy day/recovery shoe** (15% of miles)

Optionally add a race shoe for competitions.

## When to Replace Shoes

Signs your shoes are done:

- **Midsole compression** - Foam doesn't bounce back
- **Wear patterns** - Significant outsole wear
- **New aches** - Unusual soreness after runs
- **Mileage** - 500-800 km for most trainers

Track mileage in your training log.

## Carbon Plate Shoes: Worth It?

The super shoe revolution is real:

**Pros:**
- 2-4% energy savings in research
- Noticeable pop and efficiency
- Legal for all races

**Cons:**
- Expensive ($200-300 CAD)
- Limited durability
- May mask fatigue, increasing injury risk if overused

**Best practice**: Save them for races and key workouts. Don't train in them daily.

## The Bottom Line

1. **Prioritize comfort** - If it feels wrong, it is wrong
2. **Get properly fitted** - Visit a running specialty store
3. **Rotate shoes** - Multiple pairs reduce injury risk
4. **Replace regularly** - Dead shoes cause problems
5. **Don't overthink it** - The best shoe is one you'll actually run in

Once you've got the right shoes, focus on training smart. Use our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to dial in your training paces.

posts.title: Half Marathon Pacing: How I Ran 55 Minutes Faster
posts.excerpt: The half marathon rewards smart pacing. Here's the exact strategy that took me from 3:01 to 2:06 at BMO Vancouver.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Half Marathon Pacing: How I Ran 55 Minutes Faster

My first BMO Vancouver Half Marathon: 3:01:00. I had no pacing strategy. I just ran until I couldn't.

My second BMO Vancouver Half Marathon: 2:06:00. I executed the race exactly as planned. Controlled effort on the hills. Disciplined pacing in the early kilometers. Fueling on schedule.

Here's the pacing strategy that made the difference.

## The Half Marathon Challenge

At 21.1 km, the half marathon sits in a unique spot:

- **Too long** to run on pure adrenaline
- **Too short** to recover from early mistakes
- **Fast enough** to hurt from start to finish

This makes pacing critical. Get it wrong, and you'll suffer (like my first attempt). Get it right, and you'll fly.

## Pacing Strategies

### 1. Even Splits (What I Did)

Run every kilometer at the same pace:

**Pros:**
- Physiologically optimal
- Predictable, controllable
- Builds confidence through consistency

**How to execute:**
- Know your goal pace exactly (use our [Pace Calculator](/calculator))
- Check splits every kilometer
- First 5K should feel almost too easy

I aimed for 6:00/km. The first few kilometers felt slow. But I trusted the plan.

### 2. Slight Negative Split

Run the second half 30-60 seconds faster:

**How to execute:**
- First half: 10-15 seconds/km slower than goal
- Km 12-16: Settle into goal pace
- Final 5K: Let it rip

### 3. Controlled Positive Split

Plan to slow slightly in the second half:

**When it works:**
- Hot conditions
- Hilly courses
- Running for a big PR stretch goal

## The First Kilometer Trap

Km 1 will always feel easy. The adrenaline is pumping, the crowd is cheering, and your legs are fresh.

**The problem**: Going out too fast sets you up for a painful second half.

**The solution**: 
- Run 10-15 seconds slower than goal in km 1
- Let faster runners pass you (you'll catch them later)
- Consciously hold back

I let people pass me at the start. Caught most of them by km 15.

## The Middle Kilometers (10-16)

This is the "no man's land" of the half marathon:

- The excitement has worn off
- The finish is still far away
- Your pace can drift without noticing

**How to stay on pace:**
- Break it into 2-3 km chunks
- Focus on form and breathing
- Find a pacer or group running your speed
- Use landmarks as mini-goals

## The Final 5K

Km 17-21 are where races are won or lost:

- Dig into your mental reserves
- Use mantras ("Strong and steady," "I trained for this")
- Pick off runners ahead of you
- Shorten your stride if you're struggling

This is what you trained for. The discomfort is temporary.

## Race-Day Execution Checklist

### Before the Race:
- [ ] Know your goal pace per km
- [ ] Know the course (hills, turns, aid stations)
- [ ] Set watch to show current pace
- [ ] Have fueling plan ready

### During the Race:
- [ ] Start conservatively
- [ ] Check pace at each km marker
- [ ] Take fuel at planned intervals
- [ ] Stay calm if you're off pace

### Mental Cues:
- Km 1-7: "Easy, controlled, relaxed"
- Km 8-15: "Smooth, steady, patient"
- Km 16-21: "Strong, focused, finish"

## Adjusting for Conditions

### Hilly Course

This was key for BMO. I analyzed the course beforehand with [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder):
- Adjust effort, not pace, on hills
- Don't surge on downhills
- Accept slower splits on climbs, faster on descents

### Hot Weather
- Slow goal pace by 1-2% per 10°C above 15°C
- Start even more conservatively
- Focus on hydration and electrolytes

## The Bottom Line

The half marathon rewards patience and punishes aggression. Trust your fitness, start conservatively, and race the second half.

That 55-minute drop wasn't luck. It was data, consistency, and executing a plan.

Plug your goal time into our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to get your km splits, review your course on [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder), and plan your [fueling strategy](/fuel).

Then go execute.

posts.title: Strength Training for Runners: What I Should Have Done
posts.excerpt: I skipped strength training and got knee pain. Learn from my mistake. Here's the essential guide—no gym required.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Strength Training for Runners: What I Should Have Done

During my 24-week training block, I dealt with nagging knee pain. Probably from skipping strength training.

Don't be like me. Strength training makes you faster and keeps you healthy. Here's how to do it without spending hours in the gym.

## Why Runners Need Strength Training

Research consistently shows strength training:

1. **Improves running economy** by 2-8%
2. **Reduces injury risk** by up to 50%
3. **Increases power output** for hills and sprints
4. **Maintains muscle mass** during high mileage

Yet most runners skip it. I did. I paid for it with knee pain that lingered for weeks.

## How Much Is Enough?

Good news: You don't need much.

- **2 sessions per week** (20-30 minutes each)
- **Focus on key movements** (not isolation exercises)
- **Progressive overload** (gradually increase difficulty)

This is enough to see significant benefits.

## The Essential Exercises

### Lower Body (The Foundation)

#### 1. Single-Leg Squats (Pistol Progression)

Running is a single-leg sport. Train that way.

**Progression:**
1. Box step-downs
2. Assisted single-leg squats
3. Full pistol squats

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 8-10 each leg

#### 2. Romanian Deadlifts (RDLs)

Strengthens hamstrings and glutes—your power generators.

**Form cues:**
- Soft knee bend
- Hinge at hips
- Feel stretch in hamstrings
- Squeeze glutes at top

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 10-12

#### 3. Calf Raises

Your calves absorb 6-8x body weight with each stride.

**Variations:**
- Straight leg (gastrocnemius)
- Bent knee (soleus)
- Single leg for added challenge

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 15-20

#### 4. Glute Bridges / Hip Thrusts

Weak glutes = inefficient running = injuries (like my knee).

**Progression:**
1. Two-leg glute bridge
2. Single-leg glute bridge
3. Hip thrust with weight

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 12-15

### Core (Stability and Power Transfer)

#### 5. Plank Variations

Core stability prevents energy leaks.

**Progression:**
1. Standard plank (30-60 sec)
2. Side plank (30-45 sec each side)
3. Plank with leg lift
4. Dead bugs

#### 6. Bird Dogs

Coordination and anti-rotation strength.

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 10 each side

### Upper Body (Often Neglected)

#### 7. Push-Ups

Arm drive matters, especially late in races.

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 10-15

#### 8. Rows (Any Variation)

Posture support for long runs.

**Sets/Reps**: 3 x 10-12

## Sample Weekly Routine

### Session A (20-25 min)

| Exercise | Sets x Reps |
|----------|------------|
| Single-leg squat | 3 x 8 each |
| Romanian deadlift | 3 x 10 |
| Push-ups | 3 x 12 |
| Plank | 3 x 45 sec |

### Session B (20-25 min)

| Exercise | Sets x Reps |
|----------|------------|
| Hip thrust | 3 x 12 |
| Calf raises | 3 x 15 |
| Rows | 3 x 10 |
| Bird dogs | 3 x 10 each |

## When to Strength Train

**Best timing:**
- After easy runs (not before hard workouts)
- On rest days (if energy allows)
- Morning if you run in the evening

**Avoid:**
- Before long runs or speed work
- When heavily fatigued
- Less than 6 hours before a key run

## Common Mistakes

### 1. Skipping It Entirely

This was me. Don't do this.

### 2. Too Much, Too Soon

Soreness that affects running defeats the purpose. Start light.

### 3. Wrong Timing

Heavy lifting before a key run = compromised workout.

### 4. Ignoring Single-Leg Work

Running is single-leg. Your training should be too.

## Equipment You Need

**Minimal:**
- Your body weight
- A step or box

**Nice to have:**
- Resistance bands
- Dumbbells (7-15 kg)
- Foam roller

**Not necessary:**
- Gym membership
- Fancy machines
- Heavy barbells (for most runners)

## The Bottom Line

Strength training is the highest-ROI activity most runners skip. Two 20-minute sessions per week will:

- Make you faster
- Keep you healthy
- Extend your running career

I learned this the hard way. My knee reminded me every run for weeks. Start with bodyweight exercises, progress gradually, and be consistent.

Now get your training paces dialed in with our [Pace Calculator](/calculator) and combine smart running with smart strength work. Your future self will thank you.

posts.title: How to Taper Without Losing Your Mind
posts.excerpt: The taper feels wrong—less running when race day is coming? Here's how to reduce volume while staying sharp.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# How to Taper Without Losing Your Mind

The taper feels wrong. You'll feel restless, anxious, and convinced you're losing fitness.

You're not.

Before BMO Vancouver, I tapered for two weeks. Every instinct told me to do more. But by race day, the work was done. The hay was in the barn. I trusted the process—and ran 55 minutes faster than my first attempt.

## What Is a Taper?

A taper is a planned reduction in training volume before a goal race. The purpose:

- **Physical recovery** - Repair accumulated fatigue and damage
- **Glycogen restoration** - Top off energy stores
- **Mental freshness** - Arrive at the start line hungry to race

Research shows proper tapering improves performance by 2-3%. That's 2-4 minutes in a half marathon.

## The Science of Tapering

During heavy training, you accumulate:

- Muscle micro-damage
- Glycogen depletion
- Hormonal stress
- Central nervous system fatigue

Your body is fit but tired. The taper allows fitness to remain while fatigue dissipates.

**Key insight**: Fitness decays slowly (weeks), fatigue dissipates quickly (days). This asymmetry is why tapers work.

## How Long to Taper

For a half marathon, most runners benefit from **10-14 days**:

| Experience | Taper Length |
|------------|-------------|
| Beginner | 2 weeks |
| Intermediate | 10-14 days |
| Advanced | 7-10 days |

For a marathon, extend to 2-3 weeks.

## How Much to Reduce

The golden rules:

1. **Reduce volume, maintain intensity**
2. **Reduce frequency slightly**
3. **Keep some quality work**

### Volume Reduction

| Week | Mileage |
|------|--------|
| Peak week | 100% |
| Taper week 1 | 70-80% |
| Race week | 40-50% |

### Sample Taper Schedule (Half Marathon)

**2 Weeks Out (70-80% volume):**
- Last long run (12-14 km)
- One quality session (tempo or race pace)
- Reduce easy day distance

**Race Week (40-50% volume):**
- Short shakeout runs (5-8 km)
- 2-3 days before: 3-4 km with 4 x 100m strides
- Day before: Rest or very easy 15-min jog

## Maintaining Intensity

This is crucial: don't slow everything down.

**Keep doing:**
- Strides (4-6 x 100m at fast pace)
- Short bursts at race pace
- One abbreviated quality session per week

**Why it matters:**
- Maintains neuromuscular readiness
- Keeps legs feeling "sharp"
- Prevents the sluggish feeling

## The Taper Crazies

Every runner experiences this:

- **Phantom injuries** - Every twinge feels like a stress fracture
- **Doubt** - "Did I train enough?"
- **Restlessness** - Too much energy, not enough output
- **Sleep issues** - Racing thoughts, pre-race nerves

**This is normal.** The taper crazies mean you care about the race and you're rested enough to have energy.

### Coping Strategies

1. **Trust your training log** - The work is done
2. **Stay busy** - But not with exercise
3. **Prepare gear early** - Reduces race-week stress
4. **Avoid new activities** - No hiking, pick-up basketball, etc.
5. **Accept the feelings** - They're part of the process

## Race Week Details

### Nutrition

- Normal eating until 2-3 days out
- Increase carbs slightly (not stuffing yourself)
- Reduce fiber to avoid GI issues
- Stay hydrated but don't overdo it

### Sleep

- Prioritize sleep 2 nights before race (not just the night before)
- Pre-race night sleep is often poor—that's okay
- Consistent bedtime all week

### Race-Day Prep

- Lay out everything the night before
- Know the logistics (start time, corrals, bag check)
- Have breakfast tested and ready
- Arrive with time to spare

## Common Taper Mistakes

### 1. Cutting Too Much

Zero running leaves you feeling flat. Keep moving.

### 2. Adding Intensity

The taper isn't for breakthrough workouts. No PRs in training.

### 3. Trying New Things

New shoes, new food, new pre-race routine = disaster. I tested everything in training—even my fuel plan.

### 4. Ignoring Mental Prep

Visualize the race. Review your [pacing plan](/calculator). Know your fueling strategy from the [Fuel Planner](/fuel).

## The Day Before

- Short shakeout (0-20 minutes, very easy)
- Rest and stay off your feet
- Eat familiar foods
- Prepare race morning breakfast
- Set multiple alarms
- Trust that you're ready

## The Bottom Line
